subject: Re: Range of CTF and Motion for tilt-series pre-processing
body:
Just run the CTF estimation for tilt angles of a tilt series all together.
The CTF fit should work well even on a large range.

Only your most recent image had a good fit. It looks much better. 
(The extra circles in the CTF model are just a kind of visual illusion ^^ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VEb9uw8PXZU <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VEb9uw8PXZU>)

The Range still includes too much high frequency noise. Pull the high frequency limit of the range back to where the Thon rings (blue line oscillations) fade. You can see where the grey line (score of the fit) drops down. You can end the Range around there.

Then check that some (or all) of your tilt frames have a good fit like that one, and you can move on.
Warp will do another CTF estimation at a later stage called Tilt series pre-processing, where the tilt angle is considered.
 
Good luck
Teige


> On 24 Mar 2021, at 23:56, yuqi zhang <824586412@qq.com> wrote:
> 
> And is there a method of command lines to run the CTF estimate? So I can run different tilt angle(low or high) of micrographs, respectively. I thought that the tilt angle could affect the CTF estimation seriously.
> 
> btw, there are also non-concentric circle in the up-left figure. Did they affect the results? Maybe, no ?
> 
> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午10:45:13<yuqi zhang> 写道：
> I thought picture like this may better? I specified 100A - 4 A. And maybe the high frequency was remote from the x-axis. I wanted to know there were more suggestion for it. And was it good to use different range in different micrographs in a same tomogram tilt series?
> 
> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午10:28:35<yuqi zhang> 写道：
> Thank you very very much. And another question was: did the amplitude met the trouble, maybe? So the phase seemed like good. I didn't know how to deal with it. And maybe I didn't have to specify the end of the range of CTF so high, did I?
> 
> And I also met some situation like picture2. Any suggestion?
> 
> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午10:08:25<teig...@gmail.com <>> 写道：
> “If you’re fractionating the dose in very fine steps” 
> means, if e-/px/frame is much lower than 1 (I think).
> 
> “N can be lower than the frame count to avoid overfitting”
> means, you can sum together groups of frames. 1*1*4 in your case means each 2 frames are summed together.
> And, that low-dose, small pixel-size movie frames contain a lot of noise that can determine the motion fitting and give a bad result = overfitting.
> 
> In your case, your pixel size is not so small. And your dose rate is not so small, 1.59 e-/px/frame. 
> So, you could try using N=8 to use your frames individually, if you like. 
> If your tilt series has a lot of nice sample in it, like the image you shared, then motion correction is likely to be reliable. If there is no sample and few gold beads, it will struggle.
> 
> Your CTF fit was clearly a problem, but your motion correction might be fine.
> 
> Good luck
> Teige
> 
> 
>> On 24 Mar 2021, at 22:41, yuqi zhang <8245...@qq.com <>> wrote:
>> 
> 
>> 
>> Uh, there was another question about the motion model. I have 8 frames in one stack. I should specify the parameter: 1*1*8, right? What does the tutorial mean: If you’re fractionating the dose in very fine steps and collecting with a small pixel size, N can be lower than the frame count to avoid overfitting.
>> 
>> Sorry, I was so sorry that there were many paragraphs at a time.
>> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午9:14:26<yuqi zhang> 写道：
>> I forgot to upload the picture about one of CTF results. It was what I said in the CTF question above.
>> 
>> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午9:11:04<yuqi zhang> 写道：
>> Uh, there was another question about the motion model. I have 8 frames in one stack. Should the 1*1*4 be changed?
>> 
>> 
>> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午9:09:24<yuqi zhang> 写道：
>> Thank you for your very very patient reply. And I have some simple questions. I wanted to how to deal with the strange 1D curve near 0 frequency. It was caused by nanogold? And I wanted to know whether the fit of FFT zero between estimated(up-left) and experimental(down) results was in the same size. Because I thought they didn't fit at all. Maybe I should change the window size.
>> 
>> And Another question was: the first drop to zero, according to you, was the period near 0 frequency or the middle of the grey range. 
>> 
>> Is there any hot-key to PgUp or PgDn between different ctf results?
>> 
>> 在2021年3月24日星期三 UTC+8 下午1:24:52<teig...@gmail.com <>> 写道：
>> Hi Yuqi,
>> 
>> This is data from a tilt series, right, hence the exposure per frame is quite low and pixel size quite large.
>> 
>> Is your problem that the defocus and estimated resolution values in the Overview are much higher than you expected?
>> For tilt series, if the data is good and the tilt series was tracked well during collection, you would expect the defocus to be stable for each tilt series, and the estimated resolution and average motion should slightly increase as the tilt angle increases.
>> Yours doesn’t look like that, but I don’t know what kind of tilt scheme or anything about this data.
>> 
>> 
>> For CTF, I would suggest:
>> Click on the Fourier Space tab at the top.
>> Look at the fit of CTF to the power spectrum of individual micrographs.
>> Does the fit look correct to you?
>> If the fit looks good, then your data is just high defocus and low dose, a tilt frame has only a fraction of the signal as a single tilt movie.
>> If the fit looks wrong, let’s correct it:
>> The 1D power spectrum shows you the Range in grey. If you put your mouse cursor over the plot it will tell you the wavelength in Å.
>> Adjust your range so that it starts just before the first drop to zero, and finishes where the Thon rings disappear.
>> Click Process Only This Item’s CTF at the top, and you can keep changing your settings
>> Increase Window size further to get some more signal in to your power spectrum.
>> Try adjusting Range and Defocus ranges until the fit seems correct - it shouldn’t take long, just make the range suitable so the fit doesn’t do something crazy. 
>> You can check your new settings are good for a few micrographs, then process all of them when you are happy.
>> 
>> For motion, the online tutorial http://www.warpem.com/warp/?page_id=185 <http://www.warpem.com/warp/?page_id=185> says:
>> The default range of 0.05–0.25 [Ny] is usually ok for data with 1.00–1.35 Å/px. For larger pixels, consider increasing the range.
>> The more negative the B-factor, the less the higher spatial frequencies will be considered.
>> So because you have slightly large pixel size and not so much high frequency info, you could try slightly increasing the range to low frequencies and more negative B-factor.
>> 
>> Your exposure rate on the camera pixel is 0.36e/Å2/frame * 4.41Å2/px = 1.59e/px/frame (am I right?) so your motion setting of 1x1x4 seems fine if there are 4 frames in each movie. I don’t have enough experience to say whether combining frames (i.e. 1x1x2) would help or hinder.
>> Finally, you can use the Real Space tab and click on the box to show motion traces. I think it is hard to tell if the motion correction is working well, but you can have a look.
>> 
>> All the best
>> Teige
>> 
>> 
>> 
>>> On 24 Mar 2021, at 12:45, yuqi zhang <8245...@qq.com <>> wrote:
>>> 
>> 
>>> Hi, all. I have a simple question. When I do the CTF and motion for tilt-series pre-processing. I didn't know how to specify the range of CTF and motion (in Ny or A). The result values of estimated resolution and defocus, in my opinion, may be a little bad. The detector was K3 and the pixel size was 2.1A. 
>>> 
>>> The settings was in the picture. And I wanted to know whether I should change the parameter(CTF range / Motion Consider / weight with B / Models, spacially these four, I thought.)
>>> 
>>
